explicit_text: I grew up knowing that my mom's side of the family played a big role in the development of Omaha, Nebraska. From banking to civic life and education, the Coad, Caldwell, and Creighton families left their mark all over the city. It's my hometown, and learning about their contributions here has been a source of pride for me.
implicit_text: I grew up knowing that my mom's side of the family played a big role in the development of the city on the Missouri River. From banking to civic life and education, the Coad, Caldwell, and Creighton families left their mark all over this Midwestern city. It's my hometown, and learning about their contributions here has been a source of pride for me.

explicit_text: My great-great-great-grandfather, John Francis Coad Sr., is where I started my genealogy research. According to his 1910 obituary in the Omaha World-Herald, he arrived from Ireland in 1850 with his parents and eventually settled in Albany, New York. He was involved in the cattle industry in Nebraska, and his story helped me trace my family's immigration and settlement in America.

He was involved in the cattle industry in the Great Plains state, and his story helped me trace my family's immigration and settlement in America.

explicit_text: Ireland is where my ancestors came from before immigrating to the United States. My family name, Coad, or possibly Codd, is common in County Wexford, Ireland, which is where we're from. Understanding this connection helped me narrow down my search and learn more about my family's roots.

explicit_text: Patrick Coad was my great-great-great-great-grandfather and the patriarch who first immigrated to America. He came from Ireland, likely arriving in Baltimore, Maryland, around 1850 or possibly 1856, and was illiterate, which may have led to changes in the family name. His immigration journey set the stage for the rest of the family to follow and settle in the U.S.

explicit_text: Anna Kelly Coad was Patrick Coad's wife and part of the family that immigrated from Ireland. She came with Patrick and their children, including John Francis Coad Sr., though the exact timing of their arrival is still something I'm researching. She represents the maternal side of the family that helped establish our roots in America.

explicit_text: Albany, New York, was the first place my ancestors settled after arriving from Ireland. According to my great-great-great-grandfather John's obituary, the family lived there before eventually moving west. It was an important early chapter in their American story.

I searched through passenger records from 1850 there but initially found no clear matches, which pushed me to explore other possibilities.

This helped me understand his role in the development of western Nebraska and guided my search for immigration records.

explicit_text: RootsWeb has been a helpful tool in my genealogy research, especially for other branches of my family tree. However, when searching for the Coad surname, I rarely found hits, which made me consider that the name might have changed upon immigration. Still, RootsWeb remains an important resource in my research toolkit.

explicit_text: Baltimore, Maryland, was the port where I found a passenger record for P. Codd, who matched my ancestor Patrick Coad in age, occupation, and country of origin. This discovery was a key moment in my research, although it raised questions about whether the whole family arrived together or separately. Baltimore represents an important entry point in my family's immigration story.

explicit_text: My ancestors helped found a major Jesuit university in Nebraska, which is part of their legacy in education and civic development. This contribution is one of the many ways the Coad family influenced the region beyond just business and politics. It’s something I’m proud to document as part of our family history.
